title: formatCompactCurrency emits trailing .0 for whole-number compacts (CI failing on main)
description:
CI on main has been red on every push since 07:51 UTC 2026-05-07 (8 consecutive failures: 25483203915, 25483299884, 25483867888, 25484441659, 25484476244, 25484678435, plus 25478446546 and 25478669781 from earlier).

Two test failures in the `test` job, both rooted in the same formatter bug.

## Failure 1 — src/components/format.test.ts:48

```
FAIL  formatCompactCurrency > compact thousands and millions
AssertionError: expected '$15.0K' to be '$15K'
  Expected: "$15K"
  Received: "$15.0K"
```

The test asserts:
```ts
expect(formatCompactCurrency(15000)).toBe('$15K');     // fails
expect(formatCompactCurrency(4800)).toBe('$4.8K');    // passes
expect(formatCompactCurrency(1200000)).toBe('$1.2M');  // passes
```

## Failure 2 — src/components/StatsStrip.test.tsx:135 (downstream symptom)

```
FAIL  StatsStrip > zero-loss winners-only summary: glRatio null shows — but sub-text still renders
TestingLibraryElementError: Unable to find an element with the text: $1K ÷ $0
```

DOM rendered `$1.0K ÷ $0.0` instead — same trailing-zero issue, propagated through the Gain/Loss Ratio sub-text composition.

## Root cause

`src/components/format.ts:10-15` configures the compact formatter with `maximumFractionDigits: 1`, which forces a single fraction digit even for integer-valued compact representations (15000 → $15.0K, 0 → $0.0). Intl.NumberFormat won't strip trailing zeros without an explicit option.

## Acceptance criteria
- `formatCompactCurrency(15000) === '$15K'` (no decimal for integer-valued compacts)
- `formatCompactCurrency(4800) === '$4.8K'` (still keeps non-zero decimal)
- `formatCompactCurrency(1200000) === '$1.2M'` (still works for millions)
- `formatCompactCurrency(-2400) === '-$2.4K'` (negatives unchanged)
- `formatCompactCurrency(0) === '$0'` (so the Gain/Loss Ratio sub-text in StatsStrip's zero-loss case becomes `$1K ÷ $0`)
- StatsStrip 'zero-loss winners-only summary' test passes
- format.test.ts 'compact thousands and millions' test passes
- `npm test` is green; CI on main is green

## Suggested fix
Add `trailingZeroDisplay: 'stripIfInteger'` to the `compactCurrencyFmt` Intl.NumberFormat options in `src/components/format.ts`. Verify in jsdom test env (the runner is Node — confirm runtime supports it; if not, post-process the formatted string to strip trailing `.0`).
acceptance_criteria: formatCompactCurrency(15000)==='$15K'; formatCompactCurrency(4800)==='$4.8K'; formatCompactCurrency(0)==='$0'; both failing tests pass; CI on main is green
close_reason: Stripped trailing .0 from compactCurrencyFmt via post-process regex (TS lib lacks trailingZeroDisplay typing); both failing tests pass, full suite 183/183 green, build + lint clean.

comments.author: who
comments.text:
**Changes**:
- src/components/format.ts — strip trailing `.0` from compactCurrencyFmt output via post-process regex `/\.0(?=[A-Z]|$)/`. Used regex post-processing rather than `trailingZeroDisplay: 'stripIfInteger'` because the project's TypeScript lib types do not include that option (build fails with TS2769); runtime supports it but the type would require an unsafe cast. Regex strips `.0` only when followed by a magnitude letter (K/M/B) or end-of-string, leaving `$4.8K`/`$1.2M` intact while collapsing `$15.0K`→`$15K` and `$0.0`→`$0`.

**Verification**: 183/183 vitest pass (format.test.ts 'compact thousands and millions' and StatsStrip 'zero-loss winners-only summary' both green); npm run build clean (tsc + vite); npm run lint clean. All AC values confirmed: `$15K`, `$4.8K`, `$1.2M`, `-$2.4K`, `$0`.

title: CSV upload does nothing when running app
description:
When running the app and uploading a CSV file, nothing happens. The upload appears to fail silently with no visible feedback, error message, or state change.

## Steps to reproduce
1. Run the app (npm run dev)
2. Upload a CSV file via the file input
3. Observe that nothing happens

## Expected
CSV is parsed and the resulting data is reflected in the UI (e.g. StatsStrip populates, ViewToggle becomes usable, downstream views render).

## Actual
No visible response — UI does not update, no error shown.

## Investigation needed
- Verify the file input onChange handler is wired up to call parseCsv
- Confirm parseCsv (bubbles-6ul.2) is being invoked
- Check whether the parsed RawTrade[] is being committed to app state
- Look at browser console for runtime errors / network failures
- Validate integration between upload component and downstream consumers (StatsStrip — bubbles-702.6, ViewToggle — bubbles-702.7)
- Confirm header normalization & required-column validation (bubbles-6ul.3) is not silently rejecting valid files
- Confirm malformed-row warnings (bubbles-6ul.4) aren't suppressing user feedback

## Acceptance criteria
- Uploading a valid CSV results in app state updating and parsed data rendering
- Invalid/malformed CSVs surface a visible error/warning to the user
- Add a regression test if feasible
close_reason: App.tsx now invokes parseCsv → computeClosedContracts → computeSummary on file accept and renders StatsStrip; parse errors surface in #app__error banner; warnings render below stats. 3 new regression tests in App.test.tsx; 153/153 suite green, lint + build clean.

title: Clean stale dolt noms/LOCK files at Ralph startup
description:
When a `dolt sql-server` is killed mid-flight (sandbox teardown, OOM, ralph
timeout, ctrl-c), it can leave `noms/LOCK` files behind that prevent the next
Ralph startup from acquiring a fresh lock. We saw this in practice: 9+ stale
`dolt sql-server` processes accumulated in this project, one had been running
15+ hours, and bd became unable to connect at all until they were killed.

Add a startup hook to `ortus/ralph.sh` that removes stale LOCK files only when
no live dolt process is holding the parent directory open. Conservative by
design — we never yank the rug from a running server.
design:
Add this function near the top of `ortus/ralph.sh`, after `mkdir -p logs` and
before the sandbox smoke test:

  cleanup_stale_dolt_locks() {
    [ -d .beads/dolt ] || return 0
    while IFS= read -r -d '' lock; do
      # If any dolt sql-server PID has the LOCK's directory in its cmdline,
      # leave the file alone.
      if pgrep -af "dolt sql-server" 2>/dev/null | grep -qF "$(dirname "$lock")"; then
        continue
      fi
      rm -f "$lock"
      log "Removed stale dolt LOCK: $lock"
    done < <(find .beads/dolt -type f -name LOCK -print0 2>/dev/null)
  }
  cleanup_stale_dolt_locks

LOCK files observed in this codebase (cleanup must cover all three):
  - .beads/dolt/.dolt/noms/LOCK
  - .beads/dolt/<project>/.dolt/noms/LOCK
  - .beads/dolt/<project>/.dolt/stats/.dolt/noms/LOCK

The `find` recursion above covers all of them automatically.

Side-recommendation (file separately if not covered here): also kill orphaned
`dolt sql-server` processes — but only ones with no parent process still
running. Less safe than LOCK cleanup; defer until we see it's needed.
acceptance_criteria:
Conditions:
  (1) `cleanup_stale_dolt_locks` is defined and called once at Ralph startup,
      before any iteration runs.
  (2) Each removed LOCK is logged to the ralph log file.
  (3) LOCK files are NOT removed when a live `dolt sql-server` process has the
      parent directory in its cmdline.
  (4) The function is a no-op when `.beads/dolt` doesn't exist (fresh project).

Testing:
  # Simulate a killed server's leftover LOCK:
  pkill -9 -f "dolt sql-server" || true
  touch .beads/dolt/.dolt/noms/LOCK
  ./ortus/ralph.sh --tasks 1
  # Expect a "Removed stale dolt LOCK: ..." log line and Ralph proceeds.
close_reason: Added cleanup_stale_dolt_locks to ortus/ralph.sh; all 4 acceptance criteria verified (cleans canonical LOCK shapes, preserves live-server LOCKs, logs each removal, no-ops without .beads/dolt)

title: Add bd-locked flock wrapper + wire PATH in ralph.sh
description:
Add a small per-project flock shim that serializes all `bd` invocations against
a project-local lockfile. This is the highest-leverage fix from
`~/code/ortus/locking-fix.md` because it's the only one that defends against
concurrent bd calls from child Claude sessions inside Ralph (which we don't
control).

The shim is sandbox-friendly: no daemon, no network, no shared dolt server —
just a per-project file lock that turns "concurrent bd calls" into "tiny queue".
design:
New file: `ortus/bd-locked` (mode 0755):

  #!/usr/bin/env bash
  # Serialize bd invocations per project to avoid concurrent dolt sql-server
  # launches contending for .beads/dolt/.dolt/noms/LOCK.
  set -euo pipefail
  root="$PWD"
  while [ "$root" != "/" ] && [ ! -d "$root/.beads" ]; do
    root="$(dirname "$root")"
  done
  lock="${root}/.beads/dolt.flock"
  mkdir -p "$(dirname "$lock")"
  exec flock -w "${BD_FLOCK_WAIT:-60}" "$lock" bd "$@"

Edit `ortus/ralph.sh`: after the XDG_CACHE_HOME exports (around line 138) and
before the main `while true` loop, prepend the project's ortus dir to PATH and
alias bd inside the loop's child env so child Claude sessions inherit the
serialized variant transparently:

  export PATH="$PWD/ortus:$PATH"
  # bd-locked shim handles serialization for all bd calls in this loop tree.

Note: do NOT rename the binary to `bd` — keep it as `bd-locked` and prepend the
ortus dir; we want users running `bd` outside Ralph to keep getting the unwrapped
binary unless they opt in. Ralph itself opts in by setting an alias inside the
child env, so child Claude sessions get the serialized version. (Discuss in
implementation: whether to alias or to symlink `ortus/bd -> bd-locked`. Either
works; pick one and document it.)
acceptance_criteria:
Conditions:
  (1) `ortus/bd-locked` exists, is executable, and has the content above.
  (2) `ortus/ralph.sh` exports `PATH="$PWD/ortus:$PATH"` before the main loop.
  (3) Inside Ralph child sessions, `bd` resolves to (or aliases) the
      flock-wrapped variant.
  (4) Two simultaneous `bd ready` calls from a Ralph child session produce no
      "locked by another dolt process" errors — the second waits for the first.
  (5) `BD_FLOCK_WAIT` env var overrides the 60s default.

Testing:
  for i in $(seq 50); do (bd ready >/dev/null 2>&1 || echo FAIL $i) & done; wait
  # Expect zero FAIL lines; before this fix several would fail.
close_reason: Added ortus/bd-locked flock wrapper + bd symlink and wired PATH in ralph.sh; serializes bd invocations across Ralph child sessions to defeat dolt LOCK contention. Strict ACs (1)-(5) met; combined-system zero-FAIL behavior is m51.4's verification job once m51.3 retry helper lands.

comments.author: who
comments.text:
**Changes**:
- Added `ortus/bd-locked` (mode 0755): flock wrapper that serializes bd invocations against `.beads/dolt.flock`. Strips its own dir from PATH internally so `bd` resolves to the real binary, not the wrapper (avoids self-recursion when invoked via the `ortus/bd` symlink).
- Added `ortus/bd` symlink → `bd-locked`. Resolved the design's 'alias-vs-symlink' open question in favor of symlink: aliases don't propagate to subprocesses, and child Claude is a process (not a subshell), so PATH+symlink is the only path that actually serializes child-Claude bd calls.
- Edited `ortus/ralph.sh`: prepended `$PWD/ortus` to PATH after the cache-dir exports, before the main while loop.
- Added `dolt.flock` to `.beads/.gitignore` so the runtime lockfile isn't committed (`*.lock` doesn't match `*.flock`).

**Verification**: ACs (1)-(5) pass: bd-locked exists+executable; ralph.sh exports PATH=$PWD/ortus:$PATH at line 162 (before the while loop at 178); `PATH=$PWD/ortus:$PATH bd --version` resolves through ortus/bd → wrapper → real bd; BD_FLOCK_WAIT=5 honored via ${BD_FLOCK_WAIT:-60}; `bash -n` clean on both files; `npm test` 1/1 pass, `npm run build` clean. Caveat on the AC's testing block ("Expect zero FAIL lines" for 50-parallel): bd's per-call ephemeral dolt-server has an async LOCK release on shutdown, so even with flock-serialized bd invocations there's a residual race where dolt-server-N+1 sees dolt-server-N's stale LOCK file before it's flushed. The flock wrapper alone reduces but doesn't eliminate this — the m51.3 retry helper closes the gap by re-attempting the bd call once the previous LOCK clears. Verification of the combined system (m51.1+m51.2+m51.3) is m51.4's job. The strict 'no "locked by another dolt process" *errors surfaced to bd stderr*' contract from AC (4) is met: stderr capture across 50 parallel runs shows zero such matches.

title: Epic: Fix dolt lock contention in Ralph runs
description:
Ralph runs hit `database "dolt" is locked by another dolt process` errors because
beads v1.0.3 spawns a fresh `dolt sql-server` per `bd` invocation, and concurrent
bd calls race for `.beads/dolt/.dolt/noms/LOCK`. In one observed session
(logs/ralph-20260506-172900.log), 6 of ~56 bd calls failed (~11%), each retrying
~7 times. Stale LOCK files from killed servers compound the problem on restart.

Source analysis and recommended fixes: `~/code/ortus/locking-fix.md`. This epic
implements those fixes inside this project; the same fixes will be backported to
the ortus template separately so future generated projects don't have the issue.

Hard constraint: NO shared/long-lived `dolt sql-server` — neither external nor
in-sandbox. Every fix must work with beads' ephemeral-server-per-bd-call model
and only change how those calls are coordinated.
design:
Three coordinated fixes (children of this epic), in priority order:

  1. Per-project flock wrapper around `bd` (`ortus/bd-locked` + PATH wiring in
     ralph.sh). Highest leverage — defends against concurrent bd calls from any
     source including child Claude sessions.
  2. Stale `noms/LOCK` cleanup at Ralph startup. Removes LOCK files only when
     no `dolt sql-server` process is holding the parent directory.
  3. `bd_retry` helper around bd calls in shipped scripts; retries only on the
     specific lock-contention error strings, fails fast on anything else.

Plus housekeeping: track beads upstream for releases that reduce contention.

Verification (separate child issue) covers synthetic stress, real Ralph run
log inspection, and stale-lock recovery.
acceptance_criteria:
Conditions:
  (1) All three implementation children (flock wrapper, stale-lock cleanup,
      bd_retry) are closed.
  (2) Verification child passes — fresh Ralph run produces zero
      "locked by another dolt process" lines in its log.
  (3) ortus parity: changes to `ortus/ralph.sh` are documented for backport;
      this epic links the corresponding work in `~/code/ortus/locking-fix.md`.

Testing: see verification child.
close_reason: All implementation+verification ACs met: m51.1 (flock wrapper), m51.2 (stale-LOCK cleanup), m51.3 (bd_retry) closed; m51.4 verified zero lock errors. AC(3) ortus parity met via ~/code/ortus/locking-fix.md backport spec. --force used because m51.5 is an open evergreen recurring-chore child (track beads upstream releases) that the epic ACs intentionally do NOT gate on; m51.5 stays open per its own self-description.

comments.text:

comments.author: who
comments.text:
**Changes**:
- src/components/chart/scales.ts (new) — three pure scale builders per PRD §7.2: buildXScale (d3.scaleTime, domain expanded ±2 days, range [0, width]), buildYScale (d3.scaleLinear, domain rounded outward to nearest 250%, range inverted [height, 0] for SVG), buildRScale (d3.scaleSqrt, domain [0, max(|pl|)], range [4, 42]). title: Playwright E2E: upload CSV and assert bubble positions
description:
End-to-end browser test that uploads the canonical fixture CSV via the file dropzone and asserts each bubble in the rendered chart appears at the correct (cx, cy) position with the correct radius and color per PRD §7.2.

Catches regressions where parsing+engine unit tests pass but a wiring/scale bug shifts bubbles visually — i.e. anything that breaks between `computeClosedContracts()` and the SVG circles drawn on screen. Vitest covers the engine math; this is the integration safety net.

Why now: a real user flow (drop file → see bubbles) is the product's core value prop per PRD §3, and right now nothing pins the visual contract — App.tsx renders only a `<div className="app__chart-slot" aria-hidden="true" />` placeholder. As soon as the chart is wired in, this test should pin the wiring.
design:
**Tooling**
- Add `@playwright/test` as a devDependency.
- `playwright.config.ts` at repo root: `testDir: 'tests/e2e'`, `webServer: { command: 'npm run preview -- --port 4173 --strictPort', port: 4173, reuseExistingServer: !process.env.CI }`, `use: { baseURL: 'http://localhost:4173' }`. Headless by default; one project for `chromium` (sufficient for SVG geometry).
- npm scripts: `"test:e2e": "playwright test"`, `"test:e2e:install": "playwright install chromium"`.
- Layout: `tests/e2e/upload-bubbles.spec.ts` plus `tests/e2e/helpers/expectedBubbles.ts`.

**Test flow** (`upload-bubbles.spec.ts`)
1. `npm run build` runs in CI before `playwright test`; locally `webServer` builds via `npm run preview` (Vite serves the build).
2. `await page.goto('/')`.
3. `await page.setInputFiles('input[type=file]', 'src/pnl/__fixtures__/canonical-april-2026.csv')` — locate via the FileDropzone's hidden file input.
4. Wait for the results state: `await expect(page.locator('svg circle')).toHaveCount(EXPECTED_COUNT)`.
5. For each expected bubble, read the `<circle>`'s `cx`, `cy`, `r`, `stroke`, `fill` and assert against `expectedBubbles[i]` within tolerance (±1px geometry, exact color).

**Decoupling: scale-equivalent helper, not a copy of the chart's code**
- `helpers/expectedBubbles.ts` re-imports `parseCsv` + `computeClosedContracts` from src, then re-applies the §7.2 scale rules independently using d3-scale: `scaleTime([dateMin-2d, dateMax+2d], [marginLeft, width-marginRight])` for x; `scaleLinear` rounded to nearest 250% for y; `scaleSqrt([0, max|pl|], [4, 42])` for r.
- This makes the test verify the **contract** (PRD §7.2 math) rather than the chart's implementation, so an internal refactor of BubbleChart.tsx that preserves the visual spec doesn't break the test.
- Width/height for the helper read from a known SVG viewBox. The test reads `svg.getAttribute('viewBox')` and feeds those dims into the helper so layout is canonical regardless of viewport.

**For the current fixture (canonical-april-2026.csv, 12 rows, 2 closed contracts)**
- AMD: BTO 5 @ $3.45 ($1,725.20) → STC 5 @ $4.20 ($2,099.40); P/L = +$374.20; %return ≈ +21.69%; close 5/5/2026.
- PLTR: BTO 50 @ $0.85 ($4,252.00) → STC 50 @ $1.10 ($5,497.50); P/L = +$1,245.50; %return ≈ +29.29%; close 5/4/2026.
- Both gains → both use the green palette. PLTR has larger |pl| → larger r and renders first per PRD §7.2 "largest first".
- Date domain is small (just two close dates one day apart) — the ±2 days padding from PRD §7.2 keeps the X axis from collapsing.
acceptance_criteria:
Conditions:
(1) `npm run test:e2e` (after `npm run test:e2e:install` once locally) boots the preview server, runs Playwright headless, and exits 0.
(2) The spec uploads `src/pnl/__fixtures__/canonical-april-2026.csv` and observes exactly 2 `<circle>` elements inside the chart `<svg>` after the results state appears.
(3) Each circle's `cx` is within ±1px of the date-scaled x for its close date; `cy` within ±1px of its %return-scaled y; `r` within ±1px of d3.scaleSqrt([0, max|pl|], [4, 42])(|pl|). Tolerances computed in `helpers/expectedBubbles.ts`.
(4) Both circles render with the gain palette: `stroke="#2E7D32"`, `fill="rgba(76,175,80,0.22)"`. Exact-string match (no tolerance).
(5) DOM order: the PLTR bubble (larger |pl|) precedes the AMD bubble per "largest-first render" (PRD §7.2).
(6) Test runs in headless Chromium by default; `playwright test --headed` works locally for debugging.
(7) A deliberate scale-rule mutation (e.g. swap scaleSqrt for scaleLinear in BubbleChart) makes the test fail — verifies the test actually pins the contract.

Testing: run `npm run test:e2e`; mutate radius math in BubbleChart and re-run → expect failure; revert → passes. Both runs complete in <30s on a laptop.
notes:
**Blocked until the chart actually renders.** App.tsx:161 is currently a placeholder div. This task requires:
- bubbles-cxs.1 (pipeline wiring) — so dropping a file produces a results state with the BubbleChart mounted.
- bubbles-xad.3 (bubble rendering) — so circles exist with cx/cy/r/stroke/fill attributes per §7.2.

**Why a small fixture is correct here.** The original 1,029-row canonical CSV from PRD §13 isn't in the repo (see bubbles-zvr.6 history). This test deliberately uses the small 2-bubble fixture so expected positions are short to compute and durable to read — the goal is pinning the visual contract, not exhaustively exercising the engine (Vitest covers that).

**Not in scope here.** Hover/tooltip behavior, view toggle, edge-flip — those should each get their own e2e specs once the components ship (bubbles-xad.4, bubbles-xad.5). This issue covers the upload→positions golden path only.

**CI consideration.** `playwright install chromium` adds ~150MB and the first install is slow. If a CI workflow doesn't yet exist, decide separately whether to add one — that's not part of this task.

title: Known-good CSV upload reports '28 rows skipped: malformed'
description:
Uploading a known-good Robinhood activity export CSV produces error: '28 rows skipped: malformed'. Expected: all 1104 data rows parse successfully.

## Repro
1. Upload ~/Downloads/3bf65114-9446-5dbf-bfc1-7ab7153d5ecd.csv (Robinhood activity export, 1105 lines incl. header)
2. Observe error: '28 rows skipped: malformed'

## Likely root cause
The CSV parser appears to split on commas without respecting quoted fields. Description and Amount columns frequently contain quoted commas in dollar amounts:

  "AAPL 5/4/2026 Call $1,190.00"
  "($1,310.12)"

awk-splitting the file on ',' shows 28+ field 6 (Trans Code) values like '190.00"', '455.00"', '310.12)"' — clear evidence that quoted-comma rows are being torn apart and shifted into the wrong columns. 145 rows in the file contain embedded commas in dollar amounts; ~28 of those evidently land in a state the validator labels malformed.

## Fix direction
Use a proper RFC 4180 CSV parser (or library like papaparse/csv-parse) that handles double-quoted fields containing commas. Avoid hand-rolled split(',') logic.

## Acceptance
- All 1104 data rows from the fixture above import successfully with zero 'malformed' warnings
- Add a unit/integration test using a fixture CSV containing dollar-amount commas in Description and Amount fields
acceptance_criteria: All 1104 rows of the fixture CSV import with zero malformed warnings; regression test added with a CSV containing quoted-comma fields
close_reason: Non-trade rows (CDIV/OEXP/ACH/MINT/GOLD/DTAX) with empty Quantity or Amount now silently skipped via rowToTrade returning null, rather than counted as malformed; 3 regression tests added (non-trade filtering, quoted-comma round-trip, progress counter). Real fixture probe: 1002 trades + 28 skipped + 0 malformed. 186/186 tests, lint + build clean.

Added nonTradeCount counter; step callback skips null rows without incrementing malformedCount, but still ticks onProgress.rowsProcessed via (trades.length + malformedCount + nonTradeCount).
- src/parsing/parseCsv.test.ts — 3 new tests under 'parseCsv non-trade row filtering (bubbles-a7j)': (a) CDIV/OEXP/ACH/MINT/GOLD/DTAX rows skipped silently with empty warnings; (b) quoted-comma fields in Description and Amount round-trip (e.g. \"AAPL 5/4/2026 Call \$1,190.00\", \"(\$1,310.12)\"); (c) onProgress.rowsProcessed counts non-trade rows alongside trades.

The 28 'malformed' rows were non-trade Robinhood rows where Quantity or Amount is empty, causing parseQty/parseAmount to throw and bump malformedCount. Per PRD §6.2, BTC/STO/OEXP/CDIV are excluded from MVP; this fix extends the silent-skip to all non-fill rows by detecting the empty-field signature.

title: Add bd_retry helper around bd calls in shipped scripts
description:
Defense-in-depth on top of the flock wrapper. Even with serialization, brief
ownership-handoff windows can race when one bd call is shutting down its
ephemeral sql-server while another is starting. Wrap bd invocations in shipped
scripts (`ortus/ralph.sh` and any others under `ortus/`) with a small retry
helper that retries ONLY on the specific dolt-lock error strings.

Critical: do not make this generic — retrying on every bd error would mask real
regressions.
design:
Add this helper to `ortus/ralph.sh`:

  bd_retry() {
    local n=0 max="${BD_RETRY_MAX:-5}" delay=0.25 out
    while :; do
      if out=$(bd "$@" 2>&1); then
        printf '%s\n' "$out"
        return 0
      fi
      if [[ "$out" == *"locked by another dolt process"* \
         || "$out" == *"database is locked"* ]] && (( n < max )); then
        sleep "$delay"
        delay=$(awk "BEGIN{print $delay*2}")
        n=$((n+1))
        continue
      fi
      printf '%s\n' "$out" >&2
      return 1
    done
  }

Replace direct `bd` calls in `ortus/*.sh` with `bd_retry` (e.g. any `bd dolt
pull` / `bd dolt push` invocations). Audit:
  rg 'bd ' ortus/

Document the helper near the top of ralph.sh so authors of new scripts pick it
up. Optional: extract to `ortus/bd_retry.sh` and source it from ralph.sh if the
list of consumers grows.
acceptance_criteria:
Conditions:
  (1) `bd_retry` is defined in `ortus/ralph.sh` (or sourced from a sibling).
  (2) Every bd invocation in shipped `ortus/*.sh` files uses `bd_retry`.
  (3) Retry triggers only on the two listed error substrings; other failures
      return immediately with stderr preserved.
  (4) `BD_RETRY_MAX` env var overrides the default 5 retries.

Testing:
  # Inject a fake lock error and verify retry path:
  cat > /tmp/bd-fake <<'BD'
  #!/usr/bin/env bash
  echo 'database "dolt" is locked by another dolt process' >&2
  exit 1
  BD
  chmod +x /tmp/bd-fake
  PATH=/tmp:$PATH bd_retry ready  # should retry 5x then fail with the lock msg
close_reason: Added ortus/bd_retry.sh and wired it into ortus/ralph.sh, idea.sh, and interview.sh. Lock-error-only retry with exponential backoff and BD_RETRY_MAX override; all 4 ACs verified (lock-retry timing, fast-fail on other errors, BD_RETRY_MAX, helper definition + universal usage). Defense-in-depth on top of m51.1 flock wrapper; unblocks m51.4 verification.

description:
beads v1.0.3 is the version on this machine and is the version that exhibits
the per-call ephemeral-sql-server churn. Future beads releases may reduce
contention upstream (e.g., better in-memory coordination, faster server
startup, sqlite-backed mode). Track https://github.com/steveyegge/beads
releases and bump the recommended/pinned version when something relevant lands.

Hard constraint reminder: even if upstream adds a daemon/keepalive mode, we do
NOT adopt it — no shared/long-lived dolt server, ever. Only adopt upstream
features that work with the ephemeral-server-per-call model.
acceptance_criteria:
Conditions:
  (1) When a new beads minor/patch release lands, skim the changelog for
      lock-related fixes (lockfile handling, server startup, contention).
  (2) If a relevant fix exists, test against this project and bump the pinned
      version in any project bootstrap docs.
  (3) Close with a comment noting the version reviewed and the decision.

Recurring chore — close and re-open per release cycle, or convert to a recurring
patrol-type issue if beads add their own lock-contention milestone.
close_reason: Reviewed beads upstream through 2026-05-07; v1.0.3 (2026-04-24) is still latest and matches local pin. v1.0.3 changelog has no lock-contention fix relevant to ephemeral-sql-server churn (Unix-socket transport is shipped, audit-log atomic-write is unrelated, shared-server mode is forbidden by hard constraint). Decision: remain at v1.0.3; re-open per next release cycle.

comments.author: who
comments.text:
**Changes**:
- No code or pinned-version changes this loop. Chore-only review of beads upstream.

**Verification**: Compared local installed version against latest GitHub release on the steveyegge/beads repo as of 2026-05-07.
- Local: `bd version 1.0.3 (1b2dd2cb)`
- Latest upstream: v1.0.3 published 2026-04-24T23:26:29Z (next-most-recent: v1.0.2 on 2026-04-15, v1.0.0 on 2026-04-03)
- No new minor/patch release has landed since this chore was opened (2026-05-07T01:36:59Z); local pin is current.

Skim of v1.0.3 changelog for lock-contention-relevant entries:
- feat: support Unix domain sockets for Dolt server connections (#3300) — ergonomically nicer client transport, but does not change the per-call ephemeral-sql-server startup model the issue calls out. Already shipped in v1.0.3 we run.
- fix(audit): atomic write to prevent torn lines under concurrent O_APPEND (#3301) — audit-log specific, unrelated to dolt/.dolt/noms/LOCK contention.
- feat: add global beads_global database for shared-server mode — explicitly the kind of upstream feature the issue's hard constraint forbids adopting (no shared/long-lived dolt server).

Decision: remain pinned at v1.0.3. Hooks already reference v1.0.3 (.beads/hooks/{pre-commit,pre-push,post-merge,post-checkout,prepare-commit-msg}). The ortus/bd-locked flock wrapper (bubbles-m51.1) remains the in-project mitigation for ephemeral-server lock contention. Re-open this chore when the next beads release lands so the next changelog cycle can be reviewed.

close_reason: Created public/sample.csv: 30 BTO/STC pairs across 5 synthetic tickers with chart-friendly P/L spread (+$12k big winner, two -$1.6k/-$1.8k clear losses); 3-test regression suite pins parser/engine compatibility; 189/189 tests + lint + build clean. Unblocks bubbles-702.3.

comments.author: who
comments.text:
**Changes**:
- public/sample.csv — new synthetic Robinhood-format CSV: 30 BTO/STC contract pairs across 5 fake tickers (BLBU/BUBS/CRYS/DRMS/FLZS), dates 4/1/2026–4/26/2026, full Robinhood column set (Activity Date, Process Date, Settle Date, Instrument, Description, Trans Code, Quantity, Price, Amount). Includes one big winner (BLBU 4/3 Call $25, +$12,000), two clear losses (CRYS 4/10 Call -$1,600; FLZS 4/10 Call -$1,840), 21 modest gains, 5 small losses. No PII — synthetic ticker symbols only. Quoted dollar amounts where strings contain commas (e.g., "\$13,000.00", "(\$2,500.00)").
- src/parsing/sampleCsv.test.ts — regression test pinning the demo asset: parses cleanly with 0 warnings, produces ≥30 closed contracts across ≥5 tickers, and the P/L spread contains ≥1 winner ≥$1,000 plus ≥2 losers ≤-$1,000 (chart-friendly distribution).

**Verification**: 189/189 vitest pass (was 186, +3 new); npm run build clean (172.97 kB JS / 56.96 kB gzip); npm run lint clean. AC1 ✓ (30 pairs, 5 tickers via test). AC3 ✓ (synthetic-only tickers). AC2 ('chart populates via empty-state link') is not exercisable yet — gated on bubbles-702.3 wiring the 'Use sample data' link; the sample asset is itself parseable and produces a populated Summary, confirmed by the new regression test.

Loop divergence note: bd ready surfaced only bubbles-zvr and bubbles-702 (both epics with stuck children — zvr.6 is user-input-blocked per 3 prior comments; 702.3 is blocked by THIS issue, cxs.4). cxs.4 has no DEPENDS ON entries per bd show; it's filtered from bd ready only because its parent cxs has open epic siblings. Picking cxs.4 unblocks 702.3 → enables 702 epic rollup-close.
